_meta.description: Stable frontend curriculum payload for the SuitUp beginner teaching flow. This file is intentionally self-describing so the browser UI can consume it without a separate schema file.
stability_notes: Top-level keys are stable for Release 1: _meta, schema, units.
units is an ordered array and must be rendered in array order.
lessons is an ordered array and must be rendered in array order within its unit.
steps is an ordered array and must be rendered in array order within its lesson.
Every unit, lesson, and step has a stable string id for client-side progress tracking.
Step numbers are explicit and 1-based so the UI does not need to infer numbering.
Content is original beginner-teaching text only and does not include copyrighted NMJL hand-card text.

field_rules.id: Stable snake_case identifier. Never recycle ids for different content.
field_rules.title: Short learner-facing label.
field_rules.objective: Single-sentence learner outcome for the lesson.
field_rules.summary: Short supporting explanation for cards, previews, or progress lists.
field_rules.steps: Array of numbered instructional steps. Each step is atomic and beginner-readable.
field_rules.kind: Optional UI hint for step presentation. Allowed values in Release 1: explain, identify, action, check, note.
field_rules.estimated_minutes: Positive integer estimate for pacing only.

units.description: Ordered curriculum units for the Release 1 beginner flow.

units.title: Get the table ready
units.summary: Learn what belongs at the table and how each player prepares their own space before any tiles are dealt.

lessons.title: Set up the learning table
lessons.objective: Prepare a four-player American Mah Jongg table with the basic equipment in the right places.
lessons.summary: Start with a calm, physical setup so the table feels familiar before any turn-taking begins.

steps.title: Place the mat and seat four sides
steps.body: Lay out the mat or choose a clear square playing surface. Imagine four player positions around the table: top, right, bottom, and left.

steps.title: Give each player a rack and pushers
steps.body: Set one rack in front of each player position. If your set includes pushers, place them near each rack so each player can move tiles neatly.

steps.title: Bring all tiles to the center
steps.body: Place the full tile set in the middle area so everyone can help mix and build the walls later.

steps.title: Keep the rule card separate from the tiles
steps.body: If you have a yearly hand card or reference card, keep it nearby for later. In this beginner lesson, the goal is table flow, not memorizing hand patterns.

steps.title: Check that each player has a clear personal area
steps.body: Make sure every player space has room for a rack in front and a wall area just beyond it. This helps the table stay organized once tiles are dealt.

lessons.title: Build the walls
lessons.objective: Build one wall in front of each player so the table is ready for the deal.
lessons.summary: The walls are one of the most visible parts of American Mah Jongg. This lesson makes them feel normal and easy.

steps.title: Mix the tiles face down
steps.body: Turn the tiles face down and mix them thoroughly in the center. The point is to randomize them before building anything.

steps.title: Pull tiles toward your side
steps.body: Each player gathers a share of tiles from the center toward their own edge of the table.

steps.title: Stack tiles two high into a straight wall
steps.body: Build a wall in front of each player position using two stacked layers of tiles. Keep the wall as straight and tidy as you can.

steps.title: Push the four walls into a square
steps.body: Move the finished walls inward so they form a box shape in the center, with one wall in front of each player.

steps.title: Leave space for racks in front of the walls
steps.body: Your rack stays on your side of the table, outside your wall. Do not crowd your rack into the wall area.

steps.title: Confirm the table looks balanced
steps.body: Pause and look around the table. If each player has one wall and one rack area, you are ready to choose East.

units.title: Choose East and begin
units.summary: Learn how the game starts, who is East, and how the opening deal creates each player's rack.

lessons.title: Choose the dealer
lessons.objective: Use a simple draw to determine which player is East for the hand.
lessons.summary: American Mah Jongg starts by identifying East, the dealer position for the hand.

steps.title: Understand what East means
steps.body: East is the dealer for the hand. The other seats are South, West, and North in order around the table.

steps.title: Prepare a simple random draw
steps.body: Use an agreed random method to assign seats, such as drawing wind markers or drawing seat labels prepared ahead of time.

steps.title: Assign each player a wind seat
steps.body: Seat each player as East, South, West, or North based on the draw result.

steps.title: Mark East clearly
steps.body: Make sure everyone knows who East is before moving on. In beginner play, say it out loud so the table stays oriented.

steps.title: Remember why this matters
steps.body: The deal begins from East, and many table actions are described relative to East, so getting the seat order clear now prevents confusion later.

lessons.title: Deal the tiles
lessons.objective: Complete the opening deal so each player can rack their tiles and prepare for passing.
lessons.summary: This lesson focuses on the feel of receiving tiles, placing them on the rack, and getting ready for the Charleston.

steps.title: Start from East
steps.body: Begin the deal with East and continue around the table in order so every player receives tiles in a steady rhythm.

steps.title: Take tiles from the walls as your group uses
steps.body: Use the table's chosen dealing routine and keep it consistent for the whole hand. The beginner goal here is orderly setup, not speed.

steps.title: Place dealt tiles onto the rack facing you
steps.body: As each player receives tiles, they stand them on the rack so only that player can see the tile faces.

steps.title: Sort tiles into groups you can read
steps.body: Arrange the rack in a way that feels easy to scan, such as grouping by suit and then separating honors and special tiles.

steps.title: Do not worry about making a hand yet
steps.body: At this stage, the learner's job is simply to hold, sort, and recognize the rack. Hand selection comes later in guided play.

steps.title: Check that all players are ready to pass
steps.body: Once every player has their opening tiles on the rack and is settled, the table is ready for the Charleston.

units.title: Learn the Charleston
units.summary: Practice the opening passing sequence so it feels orderly instead of intimidating.

lessons.title: Understand the Charleston
lessons.objective: Know what the Charleston is and how to carry out the opening passes without panic.
lessons.summary: The Charleston is a structured tile-passing sequence before normal play begins.

steps.title: Think of the Charleston as organized passing
steps.body: Before regular turns begin, players pass tiles in a set sequence. This is called the Charleston.

steps.title: Choose tiles to pass from your rack
steps.body: When it is time to pass, select the number of tiles required by your table's Charleston step and place them face down in front of you.

steps.title: Pass only when everyone is ready
steps.body: Wait until all players have prepared their pass, then move the tiles together in the called direction.

steps.title: Receive before rearranging
steps.body: Bring the received tiles into your rack, then take a moment to sort again before the next pass.

steps.title: Keep the table talking
steps.body: Beginners should say the direction out loud, such as left, across, or right, before each pass. Clear talk prevents crossed passes.

lessons.title: Walk through the first-release Charleston
lessons.objective: Follow the beginner Charleston sequence from the first pass to the moment normal play begins.
lessons.summary: This guided sequence gives the learner a safe, memorable way to perform the passing routine.

steps.title: First pass: left
steps.body: Each player prepares the required tiles face down and passes them to the player on the left when everyone is ready.

steps.title: Second pass: across
steps.body: After receiving and sorting, each player prepares the next pass and sends it across the table.

steps.title: Third pass: right
steps.body: Repeat the same calm rhythm for the pass to the right: choose, wait, pass together, receive, and sort.

steps.title: Pause and reassess your rack
steps.body: At the end of the guided passing sequence, look at your rack again. The purpose is to make the rack more workable before regular turns begin.

steps.title: Know when passing is over
steps.body: Once the table's opening passing sequence is complete, players stop passing and the hand moves into normal turn play.

units.title: Play the opening turn
units.summary: Transition from setup into live play by learning the rhythm of a normal turn and the first discard.

lessons.title: Take the first turn
lessons.objective: Recognize the basic turn rhythm: draw, evaluate, and discard to continue the hand.
lessons.summary: The first turn is where the game stops feeling like setup and starts feeling playable.

steps.title: Begin normal play after the Charleston
steps.body: When the passing sequence is finished, the hand moves into regular turn-taking.

steps.title: Draw the next tile when it is your turn
steps.body: The active player takes a tile in turn according to the table's draw order and adds it to the rack.

steps.title: Compare the new tile to the rack
steps.body: Take a breath and look at whether the drawn tile helps what you are trying to collect. Beginners should slow down here on purpose.

steps.title: Choose one tile to discard
steps.body: After evaluating the rack, place one unwanted tile out as your discard so play can continue to the next player.

steps.title: Keep your rack private and your discard visible
steps.body: Only your own rack should face you. Discards should be placed clearly so the table can see what was thrown.

steps.title: Pass the turn calmly
steps.body: Once the discard is out, the next player begins the same rhythm. That repeating pattern is the heartbeat of the hand.
